title: for loop stop conditions should be invariant
description: "for" loop stop conditions should be invariantAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmellpitfallWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?Aforloop stop condition should test the loop counter against an invariant value (i.e. one that is true at both the beginning and ending of every loop iteration). Ideally, this means that the stop condition is set to a local variable just before the loop begins.Stop conditions that are not invariant are slightly less efficient, as well as being difficult to understand and maintain, and likely lead to the introduction of errors in the future.This rule tracks three types of non-invariant stop conditions:When the loop counters are updated in the body of theforloopWhen the stop condition depend upon a method callWhen the stop condition depends
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects for loops with non-invariant stop conditions (method calls, property access that could change)
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: ForStatement condition contains method call (parentheses) or collection property access like .Count, .Length
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: comments, test code, constants, local variable references
never_trigger_on: Local variable references (not method calls or properties)
logic.reasoning: For loop stop conditions should be invariant (unchanging during iteration) for efficiency and clarity. This rule detects method calls in stop conditions (e.g., 'i < GetCount()') and collection property access (e.g., 'i < list.Count') which could change during iteration or be repeatedly evaluated. Pattern matches for loop structure with condition containing parentheses (method call) or dot-property access (.Count, .Length).
